fact: A California driver license allows you to drive on public roads.
Before getting a California driver license, applicants must pass knowledge and behind-the-wheel drive tests based on the handbook.
Drivers must have the correct class of license for the type of vehicle they operate.
Most drivers need a noncommercial Class C driver license for standard passenger vehicles.
Commercial vehicles, motorcycles, and other vehicle types require different license classes.
REAL ID application information is provided by DMV online.
A person without a California or out-of-state driver license must apply for an instruction permit before taking the behind-the-wheel drive test.
A driver license for a driver under 18 has the word provisional.
During the first 12 months, a provisional driver under 18 may not drive between 11 p.m. and 5 a.m. unless an exception applies.
During the first 12 months, a provisional driver under 18 may not carry passengers under 20 unless accompanied by a parent, guardian, or California-licensed driver at least 25.

topic: Minor commercial and paid driving restriction
fact: A provisional driver may not drive for pay or operate vehicles requiring a commercial Class A, B, or C license.
A provisional driver may drive for a medical need if they carry a physician-signed note stating the condition and expected recovery date.
A provisional driver may drive for schooling or a school activity if they carry a note signed by a school principal, dean, or designee.
A provisional driver may drive for work reasons if they carry a note signed by the employer confirming employment.
A provisional driver may drive an immediate family member if they carry a parent or guardian note explaining the reason, family member, and end date.
A parent or guardian may cancel a teen driver license by completing the DMV cancellation or surrender request form.
A driver’s health can affect safe driving.
Drivers must be able to notice hazards in different lighting, judge distances, adjust to traffic speed, and read road signs.
It is illegal to wear a headset or earplugs in both ears while driving.
Physicians must report patients at least 14 years old to DMV when a medical condition such as lapse of consciousness may affect safe driving.
Signals, horns, and headlights communicate with other drivers, pedestrians, and bicyclists.
Drivers must signal when turning, changing lanes, slowing down, or stopping.

topic: Use hand signals if lights fail
fact: If signal lights are not functioning or are hard to see in bright sunlight, drivers should use hand-and-arm signals.
The left-turn hand signal is an arm held straight out of the window.
The right-turn hand signal is an arm bent upward out of the window.
The slow-or-stop hand signal is an arm bent downward out of the window.
Bicyclists may signal a turn by holding an arm straight out and pointing in the planned turning direction.
Drivers should signal at least 100 feet before turning.
Drivers should signal before every lane change.
Drivers should signal at least five seconds before changing lanes on a freeway.
Drivers should signal before pulling next to the curb or away from the curb.
Drivers should signal even when they do not see other vehicles around.
If turning shortly after crossing an intersection, drivers should signal when almost through the intersection.
Drivers should turn off a signal when it is no longer needed.
Drivers may use the horn to let other drivers know they are present or to warn others of a hazard.
Drivers may use the horn to help avoid collisions.
Use the horn to alert oncoming traffic on narrow mountain roads when you cannot see at least 200 feet ahead.
Headlights help drivers see ahead and help others see the vehicle.

topic: Dim high beams for oncoming vehicle
fact: Dim high-beam headlights within 500 feet of a vehicle coming toward you.
Dim high-beam headlights within 300 feet of a vehicle you are following.
It is illegal to drive using only parking lights.
Use headlights when it is too dark to see from 1,000 feet away.
Use headlights beginning 30 minutes after sunset.
Use headlights until 30 minutes before sunrise.
If windshield wipers are needed for fog, rain, or snow, low-beam headlights must be on.
Use headlights when clouds, dust, smoke, fog, or similar conditions prevent seeing other vehicles.

topic: Headlights on mountain roads and tunnels
fact: Use headlights on mountain roads and in tunnels, even on sunny days.
Use headlights when a road sign states they must be on.
Use headlights to help others see your vehicle when the sun is low on the horizon.
If you see a collision or hazard ahead, you can warn drivers behind you by turning on emergency flashers.

topic: Hand signal when slowing or stopping
fact: If you see a hazard ahead, you can warn drivers behind you by using a hand signal when slowing or stopping.
If you stop because of vehicle trouble, turn on emergency flashers or use turn signals if the vehicle has no flashers.
If possible, pull off the road away from all traffic when stopped for vehicle trouble.
If you cannot get completely off the road, stop where people can see you and your vehicle from behind.

topic: Avoid stopping over hill or curve
fact: Do not stop just over a hill or just around a curve when your vehicle has trouble.
Call for emergency roadside assistance and stay in your vehicle until help arrives.
A traffic lane is a section of road for a single line of traffic.
Lane markings show drivers which part of the road to use and help explain traffic rules.
A single solid yellow line marks the center of a road with two-way traffic.
Do not pass a vehicle ahead if there is one lane in your direction and a solid yellow line on your side.
Do not pass over double solid yellow lines.
Drivers may be to the left of double solid yellow lines when in an HOV lane with a designated entrance on the left.
Drivers may cross to the other side of double yellow lines if construction or signs direct them because their side is closed or blocked.
Drivers may turn left across one set of double yellow lines to enter or exit a driveway or private road, or make a U-turn.
Two sets of solid double yellow lines spaced two or more feet apart are a barrier.
Drivers may not drive on, drive over, turn left, or U-turn across two sets of double yellow barrier lines except at designated openings.
A broken yellow line next to your lane means you may pass when safe.
A single solid white line marks traffic lanes going in the same direction, including one-way streets.
Double solid white lines mark a barrier between regular-use and preferential-use lanes such as HOV lanes.

topic: Double solid white lane change prohibition
fact: Never change lanes over double solid white lines; wait until a single broken white line appears.
Broken white lines separate traffic lanes moving in the same direction.
Ending freeway and street lanes are usually marked with large broken lines.
If your lane is marked with large broken lines, prepare to exit or merge and look for signs.
A yield line is a row of solid white triangles showing where approaching vehicles should yield or stop.
Yield-line triangles point toward approaching vehicles.
The left or fast lane is called the Number 1 lane.
The lane to the right of the Number 1 lane is Number 2, then Number 3, and so on.
Use the left lane to pass or turn left.
Use the right lane to enter or exit traffic or when entering the road from a curb or shoulder.
Before changing lanes, signal.
Before changing lanes, check your mirrors.
Before changing lanes, check traffic behind and beside you.
Before changing lanes, look over your shoulder in the direction you plan to move.
Check blind spots for vehicles, motorcycles, and bicyclists before changing lanes.
Do not let your vehicle drift into another lane.
Before changing lanes, make sure there is enough space for your vehicle in the next lane.
It is not necessary to slow down before a lane change.
Stay in one lane as much as possible and do not weave in and out of traffic.
Last-minute lane or direction changes may increase collision risk.
Once you start moving through an intersection, keep going.
If you miss a turn, keep driving until you can safely and legally turn around.
On a multilane road, the passing lane is the far-left lane closest to the center divider.
HOV lanes are reserved for carpools, buses, motorcycles, or other qualifying vehicles as posted.
HOV signs tell drivers the minimum number of people required and the hours when rules apply.
Motorcycles may generally use HOV lanes unless otherwise posted.
HOV lanes are marked with a diamond symbol and the words Carpool Lane.
Drivers must use designated HOV entrances and exits and must not cross double solid lines to enter or exit.
A center left turn lane is in the middle of a two-way street and is marked by inner broken and outer solid lines.
A center left turn lane is for preparing and making left turns or U-turns, not for regular travel or passing.
Drivers may drive only up to 200 feet in a center left turn lane.
Before using a center left turn lane, look for oncoming vehicles, signal, check blind spots, merge completely, and turn when safe.
Slow drivers on two-lane roads where passing is unsafe must use turnouts when five or more vehicles are following.
Bicycle lanes are for bicyclists only and run alongside vehicle traffic.
Bicycle lanes are typically marked by a solid white line and signs and may be painted bright green.
It is illegal to drive in a bicycle lane unless parking where permitted, entering or leaving the road, or turning within 200 feet of an intersection.
Bike lane markings include standard bike lanes, buffered bike lanes, bike routes, bicycle boulevards, separated bikeways, and shared roadway markings.
Shared roadway bicycle markings alert drivers that bicyclists can occupy the lane and help bicyclists maintain a safe lane position.

topic: Check blind spots before bike lane
fact: Drivers should check blind spots before entering a bike lane.
Motorized-bicycle riders should use caution, travel at a reasonable speed, and not endanger bicyclists.
Drivers must maintain at least three feet between a car and a cyclist.
To make a right turn, drive close to the right edge of the road.
If a designated right turn lane is available, enter at the opening.
Drivers may drive in a bike lane within 200 feet of a right turn after checking blind spots.
When turning right, watch for pedestrians, bicyclists, or motorcyclists between the vehicle and the curb.
Start signaling about 100 feet before a right turn.
Before a right turn, look over your right shoulder and reduce speed.
Stop behind the limit line; if none, before the crosswalk; if no crosswalk, before entering the intersection.
Look left-right-left and turn when safe.

topic: Complete right turn in right lane
fact: Complete a right turn in the right lane and do not turn wide into another lane.
Drivers may turn right at a red light after a complete stop unless a NO TURN ON RED sign is posted.

topic: Right turn on red arrow prohibited
fact: Drivers may not turn right on a red arrow and must wait until the light changes to green.
It is illegal to drive, stop, park, or leave a vehicle in a posted public transit bus-only lane.

topic: Cross bus lane for right turn
fact: Drivers may cross a public transit bus lane to make a right turn.

topic: Dedicated right turn lane no merge
fact: A dedicated right turn lane does not merge into another lane and allows a right turn without stopping.

topic: Dedicated right turn during red through light
fact: Drivers may use a dedicated right turn lane even if the light for straight-through traffic is red.

topic: Dedicated right turn obey curb signal
fact: If a traffic light or sign is on the right curb of the dedicated right turn lane, obey that light or sign.
Always yield to pedestrians in a crosswalk when turning from a dedicated right turn lane.
To turn left, drive close to the center divider or into the left turn lane.
Enter a designated left turn lane at the opening and do not cross any solid line.
Enter a two-way center left turn lane within 200 feet of a left turn.

topic: Left turn yield in center lane
fact: Respect the right-of-way of vehicles, bicyclists, or motorcyclists already in a center left turn lane and always yield to pedestrians.
Start signaling 100 feet before a left turn.
Before a left turn, look over your left shoulder and reduce speed.
For a left turn, stop behind the limit line, before the crosswalk, or before the intersection as applicable.
Look left-right-left and begin a left turn when safe.

topic: Left turn complete in left lane
fact: Complete a left turn in the left lane.
Keep wheels pointed straight ahead while waiting to turn left until it is safe to start the turn.
Accelerate smoothly during and after a left turn and allow the steering wheel to straighten.

topic: Left turn on red one-way to one-way
fact: A left turn on red is legal only from a one-way street onto another one-way street when no sign prohibits it and it is safe.
A U-turn turns a vehicle around to go back in the direction it came from.
To make a U-turn, signal and use the left turn lane or far-left lane.
Drivers may make a U-turn across a double yellow line when otherwise legal.
A U-turn is allowed in a residential district if no vehicles are approaching within 200 feet.
A U-turn is allowed at an intersection on a green light or green arrow unless a NO U-TURN sign is posted.
A U-turn is allowed on a divided highway only if a center divider opening is provided.
Never make a U-turn where a NO U-TURN sign is posted.
Never make a U-turn at or on a railroad crossing.
Never make a U-turn on a divided highway by crossing a divided section, curb, strip of land, or two sets of double yellow lines.
Never make a U-turn when you cannot see clearly for 200 feet in each direction.
Never make a U-turn on a one-way street.
Never make a U-turn in front of a fire station and never use a fire station driveway to turn around.
U-turns are illegal in business districts except at intersections or divided highway openings.
A left turn from a two-way street should start in the left lane closest to the middle and end in the left lane closest to the middle in the new direction.
A right turn should begin and end in the lane closest to the right edge without swinging wide.
A left turn from a two-way street onto a one-way street starts from the far-left lane and may end in any open lane if there are three or more lanes.
A left turn from a one-way street onto a two-way street starts from the far-left lane and ends in the lane closest to the middle in the new direction.
A left turn from one one-way street to another starts from the far-left lane; bicyclists may use the left-turn lane.
A right turn from one one-way street to another starts in the far-right lane and may end in any lane if safe.
At a T intersection from a one-way street onto a two-way street, traffic going straight has the right-of-way, and drivers may turn right or left from the center lane.
To brake, remove your foot from the accelerator, let the vehicle slow, and lightly press the brake until stopped.
When stopping at a limit line, do not cross over it.
When stopping behind another vehicle, leave enough space to see its rear wheels.
Highway traffic has the right-of-way when merging onto a highway.
When entering a highway, be in the proper lane on the on-ramp.
When entering a highway from an on-ramp, be at or near the speed of traffic.
Merge into highway traffic when safe and do not stop unless absolutely necessary.
Merge into a space large enough for your vehicle to safely join the lane.
Use mirrors and turn signals when merging.
Turn your head quickly to look over your shoulder before changing lanes or merging.
Leave three seconds of space between you and the vehicle ahead when merging.
Do not cross solid lines when merging or exiting.
When crossing several lanes, signal and cross one lane at a time, checking blind spots each time.
To exit a highway safely, know your exit and be aware when it is approaching.
When exiting, change lanes one at a time and check blind spots.
Signal five seconds, about 400 feet, before exiting a highway.
Make sure you are at a safe speed to exit.
When entering city street traffic from a full stop, allow about half a block or 150 feet to reach traffic speed.
When entering highway traffic from a full stop, allow about a full block or 300 feet to merge safely.
Even with a green light, do not enter an intersection if pedestrians or vehicles are blocking your path.
When turning left, do not assume an oncoming vehicle signaling right will turn before reaching you.
Before passing, judge whether you have enough space when approaching oncoming traffic, bicyclists, hills, curves, intersections, or obstructions.
To pass safely near a hill or curve, the hill or curve should be at least one-third of a mile ahead.
Before passing, look ahead for road conditions that may cause other vehicles to move into your lane.

topic: Do not pass blind hill or curve
fact: Do not pass when approaching a hill or curve if you cannot see whether traffic is approaching.
Do not pass within 100 feet of an intersection, bridge, tunnel, railroad crossing, or other hazardous area.
Do not pass at crossroads or driveways.
Do not pass unless you have enough space to return to your lane.
To pass on an open highway, signal, check blind spots, enter the passing lane, speed up, then signal and return.
You may pass on the right when an open highway has two or more lanes going in your direction.
You may pass on the right when the driver ahead is turning left and you can do so safely.
You may pass on the right on a one-way street.
Never drive off the paved or main-traveled part of the road to pass.
If another vehicle is passing or signaling to pass, allow it, maintain lane position, and maintain speed.
Parallel parking requires a space at least three feet longer than your vehicle.
Signal to show you plan to park.
Pull alongside the vehicle in front of the space and leave about two feet between vehicles.
Before backing into a parallel parking space, check mirrors and blind spots for vehicles and pedestrians.
Back into a parallel parking space at about a 45-degree angle.
A parallel parked vehicle should be parallel and within 18 inches of the curb.
After parallel parking, turn off the vehicle and set the parking brake.
Before exiting a parked vehicle, look carefully for passing vehicles, bicycles, and motorcycles.
Before straight-line backing, observe traffic and check blind spots.
Activate the turn signal before pulling up to the curb and cancel it when completed.
Back in a straight line for three vehicle lengths while remaining within three feet of the curb.
Back at a smooth, safe speed and adjust the steering wheel as needed.
When parking on a hill, set the parking brake and leave the vehicle in park or in gear for a manual transmission.
On a sloping driveway, turn the wheels so the vehicle will not roll into the street.
When parking downhill, turn the front wheels into the curb or toward the side of the road.
When parking uphill with a curb, turn the front wheels away from the curb and let the vehicle roll back until the wheel touches the curb.
When parking uphill or downhill without a curb, turn the wheels to the right so the vehicle rolls away from the center of the road if brakes fail.
A white curb means stop only long enough to pick up or drop off passengers.
A green curb means parking is limited to the posted or painted time.
A yellow curb is for loading and unloading passengers and freight, and noncommercial drivers are usually required to stay with the vehicle.
A red curb means no stopping, standing, or parking, except buses at red zones marked for buses only.
A blue curb is for disabled parking with a special placard or license plate.
Never park where a NO PARKING sign is posted.
Never park on a marked or unmarked crosswalk.
Never park on a sidewalk, partially blocking a sidewalk, or in front of a driveway.
Never park within three feet of a sidewalk ramp for disabled persons.
Never park in front of or on a curb that provides wheelchair access to a sidewalk.
Never park in the crosshatched area next to a designated disabled parking space.
Never park in a space designated for parking or fueling zero-emission vehicles unless driving a zero-emission vehicle.
Never park in a tunnel or on a bridge unless signs permit it.

topic: No parking hydrant or fire station
fact: Never park within 15 feet of a fire hydrant or fire station driveway.
Never park within 20 feet of a marked or unmarked crosswalk, or within 15 feet if a curb extension is present.
Never park between a safety zone and the curb.
Double parking is illegal under any conditions.
Do not stop or park on a freeway except in emergencies, when directed by law enforcement, or where specifically permitted.
If you must stop on a freeway, park completely off the pavement and stay in the vehicle with doors locked until help arrives.
A vehicle stopped, parked, or left standing on a freeway for more than four hours may be removed.
During a law enforcement stop, turn on your right turn signal to acknowledge the officer.
Move completely onto the right shoulder for a law enforcement stop, even if in the carpool or HOV lane.
Stop in a well-lit area when possible during a law enforcement stop.
Turn off the radio during a law enforcement stop.
Remain inside the vehicle unless directed by the officer to get out.
Roll down the window after stopping and before the officer contacts you.
Drivers and passengers should place their hands in clear view before officer contact.
Law enforcement officers generally must state the reason for a traffic or pedestrian stop before criminal or traffic-violation questioning.
An officer may delay stating the reason for a stop if doing so is necessary to protect life or property from imminent threat.
The reason for the stop must be documented on the citation or enforcement report.
If an officer requests permission to search, you may clearly say that you do not give permission, but you may not resist or obstruct a lawful search.
A driver stopped by law enforcement must produce a driver license, proof of insurance, and vehicle registration.
If the driver does not produce required documents, officers may conduct a limited search for them.
Passengers can decline an identification request unless circumstances legally require them to identify themselves.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

fact: During a traffic stop, an officer may legally require the driver and passengers to exit or stay inside the vehicle.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

fact: Only federal law enforcement officers can ask about immigration status; California state and local officers are prohibited from asking.
Drivers and passengers generally have a First Amendment right to record law enforcement interactions in public spaces.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

fact: If recording a law enforcement stop, you should immediately make that clear.
Do not reach into concealed areas for a recording device without the officer’s permission.
If recording does not interfere with lawful duties, an officer cannot confiscate or delete the recording merely because you are recording.
In general, you may deny a request to unlock a phone or provide a password, though some circumstances may differ.
Government employees cannot retaliate just because you recorded something in public.
Even if you believe your rights were violated, do not physically resist or use violence against an officer.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

fact: The public has a right to file a complaint against a law enforcement agency, and government retaliation for doing so is illegal.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

fact: A person has the right to be free from discrimination based on protected characteristics during law enforcement interactions.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: At or approaching traffic signals or signs, yield to pedestrians, bicyclists, and other nearby vehicles that may have the right-of-way.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A solid red traffic signal means stop.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A right turn at a red light is allowed only if no NO TURN ON RED sign is posted, after stopping and yielding when safe.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A red arrow means stop and do not turn until a green signal or green arrow appears.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A flashing red light means stop, then proceed when safe.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A solid yellow signal means caution because the light is about to turn red; stop if you can do so safely, otherwise cross cautiously.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A yellow arrow means protected turning time is ending; stop if safe or cautiously complete the turn if already in the intersection.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A flashing yellow light means proceed with caution; slow down and stay alert without needing to stop.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A flashing yellow arrow allows a turn after yielding to oncoming traffic; the turn is not protected.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A green signal means go, but drivers must still stop for traffic, bicyclists, or pedestrians in the intersection and must not enter unless they can clear safely.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A green arrow means go in the arrow direction as a protected turn while oncoming vehicles are stopped.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: When a traffic light is not working, stop as if all directions have STOP signs, then proceed cautiously when safe.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A WALK or walking person signal means pedestrians may cross the street.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A DON’T WALK or raised hand signal means pedestrians may not cross the street.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A flashing DON’T WALK or flashing raised hand means do not start crossing, and drivers must still yield to pedestrians.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Pedestrian countdown numbers show how many seconds are left to cross.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: At a diagonal crossing, pedestrians may cross in any direction only when the WALK signal allows it.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Sounds such as beeps, chirps, or verbal messages help blind or visually impaired pedestrians cross.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A pedestrian push button activates the WALK or walking person signal.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: If no pedestrian signals are present, pedestrians should obey vehicle traffic signals.
notes: appFactId omitted by local audit because no matching app fact exists in src/handbookFacts.ts.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Drivers must obey all warning signs regardless of their shape or color.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: At a STOP sign, make a full stop before the crosswalk, limit line, or intersection and check traffic before proceeding.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: At a YIELD sign, slow down and be ready to stop for vehicles, bicyclists, or pedestrians before proceeding.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Red and white regulatory signs communicate instructions drivers must follow.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A DO NOT ENTER sign means do not enter the road or ramp where posted.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: If driving against traffic, DO NOT ENTER and WRONG WAY signs may be posted; when safe, back out or turn around.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: At night, red road reflectors in your headlights indicate you are going the wrong way.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A red circle with a slash shows something you cannot do.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A yellow-and-black circular or X-shaped sign means a railroad crossing is ahead; look, listen, slow down, and prepare to stop.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Many railroad crossings have a blue-and-white sign explaining what to do for emergencies or a stalled vehicle on the tracks.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A five-sided sign means you are near a school and should drive slowly and stop for children in crosswalks.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A diamond-shaped sign warns of specific road conditions and dangers ahead.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: A white rectangular sign communicates important rules drivers must obey.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: Warning signs can warn of conditions related to pedestrians, bicyclists, schools, playgrounds, school buses, and loading zones.

fact: The handbook sign chart includes the No U-Turn sign or placard as a sign drivers should recognize.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: The first vehicle to arrive at an intersection has the right-of-way, and others must wait.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road

fact: Never assume others will give right-of-way; give up right-of-way when it helps prevent collisions.

section: Section 5: Laws and Rules of the Road
fact: An intersection is any place where one road meets another road.

fact: Senior drivers often have unique needs and concerns about driving.
Driving requires physical, visual, and mental abilities.
Getting lost in familiar places can be a warning sign of unsafe driving for seniors.
Dents and scrapes on a car, fence, mailbox, or garage door can be warning signs of unsafe driving for seniors.
Frequent close calls or collisions can be warning signs of unsafe driving for seniors.
Senior drivers may consider limiting or avoiding night driving, and if necessary choosing well-lit routes.
Senior drivers may consider driving during times of day when traffic is light.
Senior drivers may consider avoiding difficult intersections.
Senior drivers may consider driving only short distances or to essential places.
Senior drivers may consider avoiding freeway driving.
Senior drivers may consider installing an additional right-side mirror.
